nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral reefs throughout the Indo-West Pacific, where it lives among the branches of Acropora and other branching stony corals. Wild specimens are typically encountered at depths between 2 and 20 metres.

diet.notes: Generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped table shrimp, and prepared carnivore foods readily. In the wild, has been observed feeding on small invertebrates and zooplankton drawn from the water column over its host coral.

avoidWith: SPS-only Acropora-dominated displays where polyp nipping would be costly

summary: The Citron Clown Goby is a small, yellow-bodied reef fish that perches on branching corals in the wild. Generally peaceful and easy to feed, it produces a defensive skin toxin and has been known to nip at Acropora polyps, so it is kept with caution in SPS reef tanks.
careNotes: The Citron Clown Goby (Gobiodon citrinus) is a small yellow-bodied coral-dwelling goby, reaching 5–6.6 cm, with blue markings around the eyes and gills. Body coloration is variable, ranging from pale yellow through olive-green to dark brown depending on individual and mood. Like other Gobiodon species, it lives commensally on branched Acropora colonies in the wild and tends to perch on rockwork or coral branches in the aquarium. A tightly-fitted lid is generally advised — although these gobies are less prone to jumping than many benthic species, escape from open tanks has been reported. Reef compatibility is best described as cautious: individuals have been documented nipping at the polyps and bases of Acropora and possibly other SPS corals (stony corals with small, delicate polyps), and breeding pairs may lay eggs on coral branches, causing tissue recession. The species is generally tolerated in mixed reefs with mature, well-stocked SPS colonies, but is best avoided in SPS-only display tanks. Gobiodon citrinus is also reported to secrete a crinotoxin — a defensive skin toxin — when stressed or dying, which has been known to harm other small fish in confined quarters. Careful acclimation and prompt removal of any deceased specimen are advised for this reason. Best kept singly or as an established mated pair; individuals may quarrel with conspecifics in smaller tanks.
breedingNotes: Has been reared in captivity, though detailed hobby-scale breeding protocols are not widely documented. The species spawns adhesively on surfaces — pairs lay eggs on coral branches near their host colony, and males generally tend the eggs until they hatch.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary aquarium-husbandry source. Provided tank size (10 gal), water parameters, temperament, diet, reef-safe rating, and Acropora-nipping caveat.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family (Gobiidae), order (Gobiiformes), max size (6.6 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 2 in / 5 cm — averaged across the range 5–6.6 cm), preferred temperature (24.7–29°C, mean 27.9°C) and broader Indo-West Pacific distribution. FishBase also confirmed the toxic-mucus defense and Acropora-commensal habit.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reared in captivity but is not common in the hobby; pairs spawn adhesive eggs on coral branches.
